Title: Breeding triple-advantage cottonseed with higher yield, enhanced nutrition, and reduced toxicity by redirecting terpenoid metabolism to astaxanthin

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in transgenic red-pigmented callus, leaves, reproductive organs, and seeds; expression level positively correlated with red pigmentation and astaxanthin accumulation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, stems, petioles, stipules, bracts, calyxes, stamens, callus, embryogenic tissues, and cottonseed kernels/pigment glands
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: High-value nutritional carotenoid and antioxidant; improves functional and nutritional value of cottonseed and may improve oxidative stability of cottonseed oil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes astaxanthin accumulation in multiple cotton tissues, increases cottonseed size/weight, and reduces gossypol content in seeds
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Single-gene expression of CrBKT was sufficient to establish astaxanthin biosynthesis in cotton callus, leaves, reproductive tissues, and seeds. 2. CrBKT expression level positively correlated with red pigmentation and astaxanthin accumulation, indicating it is the key determinant of astaxanthin production in cotton. 3. CrBKT redirected carotenoid flux toward astaxanthin and its precursors canthaxanthin and echinenone, while reducing lutein, alpha-carotene, and alpha-cryptoxanthin. 4. CrBKT-overexpressing lines produced larger and heavier seeds and showed 19.9-37.2% lower gossypol in seeds, accompanied by downregulation of multiple gossypol biosynthetic genes.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable overexpression in cotton; transient expression in tobacco; qRT-PCR; HPLC astaxanthin quantification; UPLC-MS/MS carotenoid profiling; field phenotyping; seed trait measurements; inheritance analysis in T1
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown plants; tissue culture during callus/embryo regeneration; transient agroinfiltration in tobacco leaves
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: GhPSY2; GhPSY4; GhPDS; GhZISO; GhZDS; FPS; CDNC; GhCYP82D113; GhSPG; Gh2-ODD-1
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Public cotton genome and transcriptome data analyzed from Cotton Multi-Omics Database (CottonMD); RNA-seq heatmap integration; no accession reported
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for cotton biofortification and detoxification breeding; single-gene engineering simplifies deployment relative to multigene stacks and can generate triple-advantage cottonseed with enhanced astaxanthin, reduced gossypol, and larger seed size
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although CrBKT encodes a carotenoid pathway enzyme, the paper emphasizes it as the decisive single-gene engineering factor driving the triple-benefit phenotype in cotton. Endogenous cotton hydroxylase capacity appears sufficient, so extra CrtZ was unnecessary.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses two molecules of geranylgeranyl diphosphate to produce phytoene, committing flux into carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for astaxanthin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Callus and leaves in transient assays; intended for cotton tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in transiently infiltrated tobacco leaves and transgenic cotton callus carrying PSY constructs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased carotenoid accumulation with yellow or yellow-orange pigmentation, but did not outperform single CrBKT for astaxanthin accumulation and impaired cotton regeneration when constitutively overexpressed
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmPSY1 was selected as an upstream carotenoid flux-enhancing enzyme in the engineered astaxanthin pathway. 2. Co-expression with CrBKT in tobacco produced astaxanthin, but in cotton, multi-gene expression including ZmPSY1 did not necessarily increase astaxanthin beyond CrBKT alone. 3. Constitutive ZmPSY1 overexpression caused yellow carotenoid accumulation and reduced somatic embryo regeneration and survival in cotton tissue culture.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in tobacco; stable transformation in cotton callus; qRT-PCR; pigmentation phenotyping; HPLC astaxanthin analysis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cotton tissue culture and regeneration conditions
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for precursor enhancement in stacked carotenoid engineering, but constitutive expression in cotton poses regeneration penalties and may not be necessary when CrBKT alone is effective
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced regeneration efficiency, difficulty obtaining somatic embryos, and gradual death of regenerants during tissue culture
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper concludes ZmPSY1 is not required for cotton astaxanthin production under this design because endogenous cotton pathway capacity is sufficient when CrBKT is expressed.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces multiple desaturation steps converting phytoene toward lycopene in the bacterial-type carotenoid pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for astaxanthin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Intended for cotton tissues; no independent astaxanthin accumulation shown in stable cotton plants
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in transient tobacco assays and stable cotton lines carrying CrtI constructs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Alone did not cause visible red pigmentation or detectable astaxanthin accumulation in cotton plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PaCrtI was included as a heterologous desaturase to reinforce carotenoid backbone synthesis. 2. In transient assays, combinations containing BKT could support astaxanthin production, but PaCrtI alone did not induce the red astaxanthin phenotype. 3. Stable CrtI cotton plants showed no astaxanthin peak and served effectively as a non-BKT control.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in tobacco; stable transformation in cotton; pigmentation phenotyping; qRT-PCR; HPLC
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Optional auxiliary gene for multigene astaxanthin engineering, but not necessary in cotton under this study

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates beta-carotene or ketocarotenoid intermediates in the astaxanthin branch
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor pool for astaxanthin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Intended for cotton tissues; no independent astaxanthin accumulation shown in stable cotton plants
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in transient tobacco assays and stable cotton lines carrying CrtZ constructs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Alone did not produce visible red pigmentation or detectable astaxanthin in cotton plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HpCrtZ was introduced as a heterologous hydroxylase to assist astaxanthin biosynthesis. 2. In cotton, expression of HpCrtZ alone showed no visible color change and no detectable astaxanthin accumulation. 3. The study suggests endogenous cotton hydroxylases are sufficient to support CrBKT-driven astaxanthin production, reducing the need for HpCrtZ.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in tobacco; stable transformation in cotton; pigmentation phenotyping; HPLC
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Likely unnecessary for cotton astaxanthin engineering because endogenous hydroxylation capacity appears adequate
